Thus, the parallel capacitors are calculated as $$C_p=C+C+C=3 C$$

Hence,

The equivalent capacitance between $$A$$ and $$B$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{C_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{3 C}+\frac{1}{3 C} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{1}{C_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{2}{3 C} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & C_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{3}{2} C \\ \Rightarrow \quad & C_{\mathrm{eq}}=1.5 C \end{aligned}$$

content: If the charge on the capacitor is increased by 3 coulombs, the energy stored in it increases by $$44 \%$$. The original charge on the capacitor is
explanation:
Energy stored in a charged capacitor is

$$\mathrm{U}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{C}} \quad \Rightarrow \mathrm{U} \propto \mathrm{Q}^2$$

As per given condition, when, $$\mathrm{Q}_2=\left(\mathrm{Q}_1+3\right)$$,

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \mathrm{U}_2=44 \% \text { of } \mathrm{U}_1=\frac{144}{100} \mathrm{U}_1 \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{Q}_2^2}{2 \mathrm{C}}=\frac{144}{100} \times \frac{\mathrm{Q}_1^2}{2 \mathrm{C}} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{Q}_1=\frac{10}{12} \mathrm{Q}_2 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{Q}_1=\frac{5}{6}\left(\mathrm{Q}_1+3\right) \\ \therefore & 6 \mathrm{Q}_1=5 \mathrm{Q}_1+15 \\ \therefore & \mathrm{Q}_1=15 \mathrm{C} \end{array}$$

content: In the given capacitive network the resultant capacitance between point $$\mathrm{A}$$ and $$\mathrm{B}$$ is
explanation:
In the given circuit, $$C_3$$ and $$C_4$$ are in series and $$\mathrm{C}_3=\mathrm{C}_4=8 \mu \mathrm{F}$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{S}}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_3}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_4}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{S}} & =\frac{\mathrm{C}_3^2}{2 \mathrm{C}_3} \\ \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{S}} & =\frac{\mathrm{C}_3}{2} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{S}} & =4 \mu \mathrm{F} \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\mathrm{C}_5$$ and $$\mathrm{C}_6$$ are in parallel and $$\mathrm{C}_5=\mathrm{C}_6=4 ~\mu \mathrm{F}$$

$$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{C}_5+\mathrm{C}_6$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}=8 \mu \mathrm{F}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Equivalent circuit is as shown in figure.

Now, $$\mathrm{C}_2$$ and $$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}$$ are in series and their combination in parallel with $$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{S}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{E}} & =\frac{\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}}+\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}} \\ & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{(8)(8)}{16}+4 \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{E}}=8 \mu \mathrm{F} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\mathrm{C}_1$$ and $$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{E}}$$ are in series,

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad C=\frac{C_1 C_E}{C_1+C_E} \\ & \therefore \quad C=\frac{(8)(8)}{16} \\ & \therefore \quad C=4 \mu F \end{aligned}$$

content: The equivalent capacity between terminal $$\mathrm{A}$$ and $$\mathrm{B}$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\frac{1}{C_s}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C} \\\\ &\therefore \quad C_s =\frac{C}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Now, $$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}$$ and $$\mathrm{C}$$ are connected in parallel,

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{net}}=\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{s}}+\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{3}+\mathrm{C}=\frac{4 \mathrm{C}}{3}$$

content: The potential on the plates of capacitor are $$+20 \mathrm{~V}$$ and $$-20 \mathrm{~V}$$. The charge on the plate is $$40 \mathrm{C}$$. The capacitance of the capacitor is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} V & =20-(-20)=40 \text { volt } \\ Q & =40 C \\ C & =\frac{Q}{V} \\ \therefore \quad C & =\frac{40}{40} \\ \therefore \quad C & =1 F \end{aligned}$$

content: Two spherical conductors of capacities $$3 \mu \mathrm{F}$$ and $$2 \mu \mathrm{F}$$ are charged to same potential having radii $$3 \mathrm{~cm}$$ and $$2 \mathrm{~cm}$$ respectively. If '$$\sigma_1$$' and '$$\sigma_2$$' represent surface density of charge on respective conductors then $$\frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2}$$ is
explanation:
We know, $$\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{V}}$$

As both the charged spheres are at the same potential, the charge on both spheres is

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}_1=\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{Q}_2=\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

The charge densities of both spheres are

$$\sigma_1=\frac{\mathrm{Q}_1}{\mathrm{~A}_1}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{~V}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}_1^2}$$

Similiarly,

$$\sigma_2=\frac{\mathrm{Q}_2}{\mathrm{~A}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{~V}}{4 \pi \mathrm{r}_2^2}$$

Taking the ratios,

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} & =\frac{C_2 r_1^2}{C_1 r_2^2} \\ \frac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1} & =\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times(0.03)^2}{3 \times 10^{-6} \times(0.02)^2}=\frac{3}{2} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} & =\frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: Three identical capacitors of capacitance '$$\mathrm{C}$$' each are connected in series and this connection is connected in parallel with one more such identical capacitor. Then the capacitance of whole combination is
explanation:
The equivalent capacitance of three capacitances connected in series is

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{C_{e q}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C} \\ & \frac{1}{C_{e q}}=\frac{3}{C} \\ & C_{e q}=\frac{C}{3} \end{aligned}$$

This capacitance is connected in parallel with another capacitance.

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow C_{\text {total }}=\frac{C}{3}+C \\ & C_{\text {total }}=\frac{4 C}{3} \end{aligned}$$

content: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery and battery remains connected. The dielectric slab of constant '$$\mathrm{K}$$' is inserted between the plates and then taken out. Then electric field between the plates

Two identical capacitors have the same capacitance '$$C$$'. One of them is charged to a potential $$V_1$$ and the other to $$V_2$$. The negative ends of the capacitors are connected together. When the positive ends are also connected, the decrease in energy of the combined system is
explanation:
Initial energy of the combined system

$$\mathrm{U}_1=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}_1^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}_2^2=\frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}\left(\mathrm{~V}_1^2+\mathrm{V}_2^2\right)$$

On joining the two condensers in parallel, common potential,

$$\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_1+\mathrm{V}_2}{2}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Final energy of the combined system:

$$\mathrm{U}_2=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{C})\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_1+\mathrm{V}_2}{2}\right)^2$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ Decrease in energy will be:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta \mathrm{U} & =\mathrm{U}_1-\mathrm{U}_2 \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{C}}{2}\left(\mathrm{~V}_1^2+\mathrm{V}_2^2\right)-\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{C})\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}_1+\mathrm{V}_2}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{C}\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{V}_2\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If a capacitor of capacity $$900 ~\mu \mathrm{F}$$ is charged to $$100 \mathrm{~V}$$ and its total energy is transferred to a capacitor of capacity $$100 ~\mu \mathrm{F}$$, then its potential will be
explanation:
Let the unknown potential be $$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{u}}$$

Energy of a capacitor $$=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} C_1 V_1^2=\frac{1}{2} C_2\left(V_u\right)^2 \\ & \frac{1}{2} \times 900 \times 10^{-6} \times(100)^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times\left(V_u\right)^2 \\ & \left(V_u\right)^2=9 \times 100^2 \\ \therefore \quad & V_u=300 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

content: Two dielectric slabs having dielectric constant '$$\mathrm{K}_1$$' and '$$\mathrm{K}_2$$' of thickness $$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4}$$ and $$\frac{3 \mathrm{~d}}{4}$$ are inserted between the plates as shown in figure. The net capacitance between $$A$$ and $$B$$ is $$\left[\varepsilon_0\right.$$ is permittivity of free space]
explanation:
Capacity of $$1^{\text {st }}$$ Capacitor,

$$C_1=\frac{K_1 \varepsilon_0 A}{d / 4}=\frac{4 K_1 \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

Capacity of $$2^{\text {nd }}$$ Capacitor,

$$\mathrm{C}_2=\frac{\mathrm{K}_2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{3 \mathrm{~d} / 4}=\frac{4 \mathrm{~K}_2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{3 \mathrm{~d}}$$

Equivalent capacitance $$\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_1} & =\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4 \mathrm{~K}_1 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}} ; \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~d}}{4 \mathrm{~K}_2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}} \\ \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}} & =\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4 \mathrm{~K}_1 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}+\frac{3 \mathrm{~d}}{4 \mathrm{~K}_2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}} \\ \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}} & =\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_1}+\frac{3}{\mathrm{~K}_2}\right] \\ \frac{1}{\mathrm{C}} & =\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{K}_2+3 \mathrm{~K}_1}{\mathrm{~K}_1 \mathrm{~K}_2}\right] \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{C} & =\frac{4 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{K}_1 \mathrm{~K}_2}{3 \mathrm{~K}_1+\mathrm{K}_2}\right] \end{aligned}$$

content: A parallel plate capacitor has plate area '$$\mathrm{A}$$' and separation between plates is '$$d$$'. It is charged to a potential difference of $$\mathrm{V}_0$$ volt. The charging battery is then disconnected and plates are pulled apart three times the initial distance. The work done to increase the distance between the plates is $$\left(\varepsilon_0=\right.$$ permittivity of free space)
explanation:
Let the initial capacitance be $$\mathrm{C}_0=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}$$

Let the charge on the capacitor be $$Q_{\text {initial }}=C_0 V_0$$

Plate separation is increased by 3 times i.e., $$\mathrm{d}^{\prime}=3 \mathrm{~d}$$

$$\mathrm{C}_{\text {final }}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{3 \mathrm{~d}}=\frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{C}_0}{3}$$

Let $$\mathrm{Q}_{\text {final }}$$ be the final charge on the capacitor and $$V_{\text {final }}$$ be the final potential on the capacitor.

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{Q}_{\text {final }}=\mathrm{C}_{\text {final }} \mathrm{V}_{\text {final }}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}_{\text {final }} $$

As the capacitor is isolated,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}_{\text {final }}=\mathrm{Q}_{\text {initial }} \text {, } \\ & \mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}_{\text {final }} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{V}_{\text {final }}=3 \mathrm{~V}_0 \\ & \text { Work done }=\text { Final P.E }- \text { Initial P.E } \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_{\text {final }}\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {final }}\right)^2-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_0\left(\mathrm{~V}_0\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{C}_0}{3} 9 \mathrm{~V}_0^2-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0^2 \\ & =\mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0^2\left(\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & =\mathrm{C}_0 \mathrm{~V}_0^2 \\ & =\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{AV}_0^2}{\mathrm{~d}} \quad \ldots\left(\because \mathrm{C}_0=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}\right) \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: Two capacitors $$\mathrm{C}_1=3 \mu \mathrm{F}$$ and $$\mathrm{C}_2=2 \mu \mathrm{F}$$ are connected in series across d.c. source of $$100 \mathrm{~V}$$. The ratio of the potential across $$C_2$$ to $$C_1$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_1=3 \mu \mathrm{F} \text { and } \mathrm{C}_2=2 \mu \mathrm{F} \\ \therefore \quad & \mathrm{C}_{\text {series }}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_2}{\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{6}{5} \mu \mathrm{F} \\ & \text { Also, } \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{CV} \\ \mathrm{Q} & =\mathrm{C}_{\text {series }} \times \mathrm{V} \\ & =\frac{6}{5} \times 100=120 \mu \mathrm{C} \end{aligned}$$

Q will be the same across both the capacitors as they are in series.

$$\therefore \quad$$ Potential across capacitors,

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_1=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{C}_1}=\frac{120}{3}=40 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{~V}_2=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{120}{2}=60 \mathrm{~V} \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{V}_2 & : \mathrm{V}_1=60: 40=3: 2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The mean electrical energy density between plates of a charged air capacitor is (where $$\mathrm{q}=$$ charge on capacitor, $$\mathrm{A}=$$ Area of capacitor plate)
explanation:
For a parallel plate capacitor, the energy density $$=\frac{1}{2} E^2 \varepsilon_0$$

But $$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Energy density } & =\frac{1}{2} \frac{\sigma^2}{\varepsilon_0^2} \times \varepsilon_0 \\ & =\frac{\sigma^2}{2 \varepsilon_0} \\ & =\frac{\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{A}}\right)^2}{2 \varepsilon_0} \quad \ldots .(\because \sigma=\mathrm{q} / \mathrm{A}) \\ & =\frac{\mathrm{q}^2}{2 \mathrm{~A}^2 \cdot \varepsilon_0} \end{aligned}$$

content: A parallel combination of two capacitors of capacities '$$2 ~\mathrm{C}$$' and '$$\mathrm{C}$$' is connected across $$5 \mathrm{~V}$$ battery. When they are fully charged, the charges and energies stored in them be '$$\mathrm{Q}_1$$', '$$Q_2$$' and '$$E_1$$', '$$E_2$$' respectively. Then $$\frac{E_1-E_2}{Q_1-Q_2}$$ in $$\mathrm{J} / \mathrm{C}$$ is (capacity is in Farad, charge in Coulomb and energy in J)
explanation:
We know, $$\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{C} . \mathrm{V}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad Q_1=10 \mathrm{C} \text { and } \mathrm{Q}_2=5 \mathrm{C} & \\ \text { Energy stored, } \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{E}_1=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{~V}^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \mathrm{C} \times 25 =25 \mathrm{~J} \\ \text { Similarly, } \mathrm{E}_2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{~V}^2=\frac{1}{2} \times \mathrm{C} \times 25 =12.5~\mathrm{J}\\ \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{E}_1-\mathrm{E}_2}{\mathrm{Q}_1-\mathrm{Q}_2}=\frac{12.5}{5}=\frac{5}{2} \end{array}$$

content: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is $$2.5 ~\mu \mathrm{F}$$. When it is half filled with a dielectric as shown in figure, its capacitance becomes $$5 ~\mu \mathrm{F}$$. The dielectric constant of the dielectric is
explanation:
Given $$C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}=2.5 ~\mu F$$

When half filled with air,

$$\mathrm{C}_1=\frac{\varepsilon_0(\mathrm{~A} / 2)}{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \mathrm{~d}} \quad \ldots . .\left(\because \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=1\right)$$

When half filled with a dielectric,

$$\mathrm{C}_2=\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \varepsilon_0(\mathrm{~A} / 2)}{\mathrm{d}}=\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \mathrm{~d}}$$

From the figure, it can be seen that $$C_1$$ and $$C_2$$ are in parallel configuration.

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2 \\ & \text { Given, } \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=5 \mu \mathrm{F} \\ & \Rightarrow 5 \mu \mathrm{F}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \mathrm{~d}}+\frac{\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \mathrm{~d}} \\ & 5=\frac{2.5}{2}+\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \frac{2.5}{2} \\ & \frac{3.75}{1.25}=\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}} \\ \therefore \quad & \varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=3 \end{array}$$

content: The ratio of potential difference that must be applied across parallel and series combination of two capacitors $$C_1$$ and $$C_2$$ with their capacitance in the ratio $$1: 2$$ so that energy stored in these two cases becomes same is
explanation:
Given: $$\mathrm{C_1: C_2=1: 2}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_2 & =2 \mathrm{C}_1 \\ \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}} & =\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2=\mathrm{C}_1+2 \mathrm{C}_1=3 \mathrm{C}_1 \\ \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{S}} & =\frac{\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_2}{\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{2 \mathrm{C}_1^2}{3 \mathrm{C}_1}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{C}_1 \end{aligned}$$

Let $$V_P$$ and $$V_S$$ be the potentials applied across the parallel and series combinations respectively, then using $$\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}^2$$ we can write,

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{1}{2} C_P V_P^2=\frac{1}{2} C_S V_S^2 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{V_P^2}{V_S^2}=\frac{C_S}{C_P} \\ & \frac{V_P}{V_S}=\sqrt{\frac{\frac{2}{3} C_1}{3 C_1}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{3} \end{array}$$

content: The potential energy of charged parallel plate capacitor is $$v_0$$. If a slab of dielectric constant $$\mathrm{K}$$ is inserted between the plates, then the new potential energy will be
explanation:
We know, $$v_0=\frac{Q^2}{2 C}$$

On inserting the slab of dielectric constant $$\mathrm{k}$$, the new capacitance $$\mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\mathrm{KC}$$

$$\therefore \quad$$ New potential energy $$v_0^{\prime}=\frac{Q^2}{2 C^{\prime}}$$

$$v_0^1=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \mathrm{KC}}=\frac{v_0}{\mathrm{~K}}$$

content: A parallel plate air capacitor has a uniform electric field 'E' in the space between the plates. Area of each plate is A and the distance between the plates is '$$\mathrm{d}$$'. The energy stored in the capacitor is $$\left[\varepsilon_0=\right.$$ permittivity of free space)
explanation:
The intensity of the electric field (E) between two plane parallel sheets of equal and opposite charges is given by $$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$\therefore \sigma=\mathrm{E} \varepsilon_0$$ where $$\sigma=$$ surface density of charge $$=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{A}}$$

$$\therefore$$ Charge on either plate of the capacitor is $$\mathrm{Q}=\sigma \mathrm{A}=\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{EA}$$ and $$\mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}$$

$$\therefore$$ The energy stored in the capacitor is

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\left(\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{EA}\right)^2}{2 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0^2 \mathrm{~A}^2 \mathrm{E}^2 \times \mathrm{d}}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}} \\ & \therefore \mathbf{U}=\frac{\mathbf{1}}{\mathbf{2}} \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E}^{\mathbf{2}} \mathbf{A d} \end{aligned}$$

content: A parallel plate capacitor is charged and then disconnected from the charging battery. If the plates are now moved further apart by pulling them by means of insulating handles, then

options.content: the capacitance of the capacitor increases
the charge on the capacitor decreases
the voltage across the capacitor increases
the energy stored in the capacitor decreases

explanation:
For a parallel plate capacitor, $$\mathrm{C}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}}$$

When the charging battery is disconnected and $$\mathrm{d}$$ is increased then

(a) Q remains constant

(b) $$\mathrm{C} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{~d}}$$ hence if $$\mathrm{d}$$ is increased, $$\mathrm{C}$$ is decreased.

(c) $$\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{V}}$$ or $$\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{C}}$$ if $$\mathrm{C}$$ is decreased, $$\mathrm{V}$$ will increase

(d) $$\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{\mathrm{C}}$$ if $$\mathrm{C}$$ is decreased, then $$\mathrm{E}$$ will increase

Thus (a), (b) and (d) are wrong. Only (c) is correct.

content: The force between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance '$$\mathrm{C}$$' and distance of separation of the plates '$$\mathrm{d}$$' with a potential difference '$$\mathrm{V}$$' between the plates is
explanation:
The force of attraction between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor is

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}=\frac{\sigma^2 \mathrm{~A}}{2 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{\mathrm{~A}^2} \times \frac{\mathrm{A}}{2 \varepsilon_0}\left(\because \sigma=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{A}}\right) \\ & \quad=\frac{\mathrm{Q}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~V}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}(\because \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{CV}) \\ & \therefore \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{C}\left[\frac{\mathrm{CV}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}\right]=\frac{\varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{~d}} \times \frac{\mathrm{CV}^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{~A}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{CV}^2}{\mathrm{~d}} \end{aligned}$$

content: A battery is used to charge a parallel plate capacitor till the potential difference between the plates becomes equal to the e.m.f. of the battery. The ratio of the energy stored int he capacitor to the work done by the battery will be
explanation:
Energy stored in the capacitor $$\mathrm{U}=\frac{1}{2} q \mathrm{~V}$$

Work done by the battery $$\mathrm{W}=\mathrm{qV}$$

$$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{U}}{\mathrm{W}}=\frac{1}{2}$$

content: Capacitors of capacities $$\mathrm{C}_1, \mathrm{C}_2$$ and $$\mathrm{C}_3$$ are connected in series. If the combination is connected to a supply of '$$\mathrm{V}$$' volt, then potential difference across capacitor '$$\mathrm{C}_1$$' is
explanation:
In series combination, the equivalent capacitance $$\mathrm{C}$$ is given by

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{C} & =\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{1}{C} & =\frac{C_2 C_3+C_1 C_3+C_1 C_2}{C_1 C_2 C_3} \\ \therefore \quad C & =\frac{C_1 C_2 C_3}{C_2 C_3+C_1 C_3+C_1 C_2} \end{aligned}$$

Charge $$\mathrm{Q}$$ stored by the combinations is given by

$$Q=C V=\frac{C_1 C_2 C_3 V}{\mathrm{C}_2 C_3+C_1 C_3+C_1 C_2}$$

Charge on each capacitor is same. Hence potential difference across $$\mathrm{C_1}$$ is $$\mathrm{V_1=\frac{Q}{C_1}}$$

$$\therefore \quad \mathrm{V_1=\frac{C_2 C_3 V}{C_2 C_3+C_1 C_3+C_1 C_2}}$$

content: The plates of a parallel plate capacitor of capacity '$$\mathrm{C}_1$$' are moved closer together until they ant half their original separation. The new capacitance '$$\mathrm{C}_2$$' is
explanation:
The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor is given by

$$\mathrm{C=\frac{k A_0}{d}}$$

If the distance between the plates $$\mathrm{d}$$ is decreased to half, the capacitance will become double.

content: Two identical capacitors have the same capacitance '$$\mathrm{C}$$'. One of them is charged to potential '$$\mathrm{V_1}$$' and the other to $$\mathrm{V_2}$$. The negative ends of the capacitors are connected together. When positive ends are also connected, the decrease in energy of the combined system is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & Q_1=C V_1, Q_2=C V_2, Q=Q_1+Q_2=2 C V \\ & \therefore C V_1+C V_2=2 C V \\ & \therefore \quad V=\frac{V_1+V_2}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Decrease in energy } =\frac{1}{2} C V_1^2+\frac{1}{2} C V_2^2-\frac{1}{2}(2 C)\left(\frac{V_1+V_2}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} C\left[V_1^2+V_2^2-2\left(\frac{V_1+V_2}{2}\right)^2\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2} C\left[V_1^2+V_2^2-\frac{1}{2}\left(V_1+V_2\right)^2\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{4} C\left[2 V_1^2+2 V_2^2-\left(V_1+V_2\right)^2\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{4} C\left(V_1-V_2\right)^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: If the potential difference across a capacitor is increased from $$5 \mathrm{~V}$$ to $$15 \mathrm{~V}$$, then the ratio of final energy to initial energy stored in the capacitor is
explanation:

Final energy $$\mathrm{W}_2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{CV}_2^2$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{W_2}{W_1}=\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^2=\left(\frac{15}{5}\right)^2=(3)^2=9$$

content: Two parallel plates with dielectric placed between the plates are as shown in figure. The resultant capacity of capacitor will be [A = area of plate. $$t_1, t_2$$ and $$t_3$$ are thickness of dielectric slabs, $$\mathrm{k}_1, \mathrm{k}_2$$ and $$\mathrm{k}_3$$ are dielectric constants.
explanation:
In the absence of the dielectric the electric field between the plates is given by

$$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{A} \varepsilon_0}$$

when dielectric is placed, the field gets reduced by the factor $$k$$.

The potential difference between the plates is given by

$$\begin{aligned} V & =E_1 t_1+E_2 t_2+E_2 t_3 \\ & =\frac{E}{k_1} \cdot t_1+\frac{E}{k_2} \cdot t_2+\frac{E}{k_3} \cdot t_3 \\ & =E\left(\frac{t_1}{k_1}+\frac{t_2}{k_2}+\frac{t_3}{k_3}\right) \\ & =\frac{q}{A \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{t_1}{k_1}+\frac{t_2}{k_2}+\frac{t_3}{k_3}\right) \\ C & =\frac{q}{V}=\frac{A \varepsilon_0}{\left(\frac{t_1}{k_1}+\frac{t_2}{k_2}+\frac{t_3}{k_3}\right)} \end{aligned}$$

content: In parallel plate capacitor, electric field between the plates is '$$E$$'. If the charge on the plates is '$$Q$$' then the force on each plate is

content: If the charge on the capacitor is increased by 2 C the energy stored in it increased by 21%. Total original charge on the capacitor is
explanation:
Let's start by recalling the formula for the energy stored in a capacitor. The energy $U$ stored in a capacitor with capacitance $C$ and charge $Q$ is given by:

$$U = \frac{Q^2}{2C}$$

Since we do not know the capacitance $C$, we will manipulate the formula using given information. If the charge on the capacitor is increased by 2 C, the new charge $Q'$ becomes $Q + 2$. Thus, the new energy $U'$ becomes:

$$U' = \frac{(Q + 2)^2}{2C}$$

We are given that this new energy is 21% greater than the original energy. Therefore, we can write:

$$U' = 1.21U$$

Substituting the expressions for $U$ and $U'$, we get:

$$\frac{(Q + 2)^2}{2C} = 1.21 \cdot \frac{Q^2}{2C}$$

Since $C$ is a common factor in the numerator and denominator, it cancels out. Hence, we can simplify to:

$$ (Q + 2)^2 = 1.21Q^2 $$

Expanding and rearranging the equation, we obtain:

$$ Q^2 + 4Q + 4 = 1.21Q^2 $$

$$ 0.21Q^2 - 4Q - 4 = 0 $$

Solving this quadratic equation using the quadratic formula, where $a = 0.21$, $b = -4$, and $c = -4$, we get:

$$ Q = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} $$

Substitute the values of $a$, $b$, and $c$:

$$ Q = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{(-4)^2 - 4 \cdot 0.21 \cdot (-4)}}{2 \cdot 0.21} $$

$$ Q = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 + 3.36}}{0.42} $$

$$ Q = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{19.36}}{0.42} $$

$$ Q = \frac{4 \pm 4.4}{0.42} $$

Therefore, we have two possible solutions for $Q$:

$$ Q = \frac{4 + 4.4}{0.42} = 20 $$

$$ Q = \frac{4 - 4.4}{0.42} = -0.95 \ (not \ practical) $$

Since a negative charge is not practical in this context, the original charge on the capacitor is:

Option C: 20 C

content: When the battery across the plates of a charged condenser is disconnected and a dielectric slab is introduced between its plates then the energy stored
explanation:
To understand what happens to the energy stored in a charged capacitor when a dielectric slab is introduced between its plates (after disconnecting the battery), let's break down the concepts involved.

A capacitor stores energy in the electric field between its plates. The energy stored in a capacitor is given by the formula:

$$ U = \frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C} $$

where:

  • U is the energy stored.
  • Q is the charge on the capacitor.
  • C is the capacitance of the capacitor.

The capacitance of a capacitor is given by:

$$ C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} $$

where:

  • $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space.
  • A is the area of the plates.
  • d is the distance between the plates.

When a dielectric slab with dielectric constant κ is introduced between the plates, the new capacitance C' becomes:

$$ C' = \kappa C $$

Since the battery is disconnected, the charge Q on the capacitor remains constant. Let's understand the impact of the increased capacitance (due to the dielectric slab) on the stored energy.

The new energy stored U' can be expressed as:

$$ U' = \frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C'} $$

Substituting $C' = \kappa C$, we get:

$$ U' = \frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{\kappa C} $$

This equation shows that:

$$ U' = \frac{U}{\kappa} $$

We know that $\kappa > 1$, therefore:

$$ U' < U $$

This indicates that the energy stored in the capacitor decreases when a dielectric slab is introduced between its plates (after disconnecting the battery).

So, the correct answer is:

Option D: decreases

content: A condenser of capacity '$$\mathrm{C}_1$$' is charged to potential '$$\mathrm{V}_1$$' and then disconnected. Uncharged capacitor of capacity '$$\mathrm{C}_2$$' is connected in parallel with '$$\mathrm{C}_1$$'. The resultant potential '$$\mathrm{V}_2$$' is

content: A parallel plate air capacitor is charged upto 100 V. A plate 2 mm thick is inserted between the plates. Then to maintain the same potential difference, the distance between the plates is increased by 1.6 mm. The dielectric constant of the thick plate is
explanation:
If d is the initial distance between the plates then capacitance is given by $$\mathrm{C=\frac{kA \in_0}{d}}$$

When a plate of thickness t = 2 mm is inserted between the plates the capacitance,

$$C_1=\frac{k A \in_0}{\left(d-t+\frac{t}{k}\right)}$$

If distance between the plates is increased by $$\mathrm{x}=1.6 \mathrm{~mm}$$

Capacitance becomes, $$C_2=\frac{\mathrm{kA} \in_0}{\left(d+x-t+\frac{t}{k}\right)} \quad$$ But $$C_2=C$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \mathrm{d}+\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{t}+\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{d} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{t}-\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \therefore 2-1.6=\frac{2}{\mathrm{k}} \\ & \therefore 0.4=\frac{2}{\mathrm{k}} \quad \therefore \mathrm{k}=5\end{aligned}$$

content: Five capacitors each of capacity $$C$$ are connected as shown in figure. If their resultant capacity is $$2 \mu \mathrm{F}$$, then the capacity of each condenser is
explanation:
Given that the resultant capacitance $ C_{\text{eq}} = 2 \, \mu \text{F} $:

The capacitors are connected in series. Therefore, the formula for the equivalent capacitance of capacitors in series is:

$ \frac{1}{C_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{C} + \frac{1}{C} $

This simplifies to:

$ \frac{1}{C_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{5}{C} $

Substitute the given equivalent capacitance:

$ \frac{1}{2 \, \mu \text{F}} = \frac{5}{C} $

Solving for $ C $:

$ C = 10 \, \mu \text{F} $

Therefore, each capacitor has a capacitance of $ 10 \, \mu \text{F} $.

content: The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with air as medium is $$3 \mu \mathrm{F}$$. With the introduction of a dielectric medium between the plates, the capacitance becomes $$15 \mu \mathrm{F}$$. The permittivity of the medium in $$\mathrm{SI}$$ unit is $$[\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{SI}$$ unit]
explanation:
The capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor with air is

$$C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

where, $$\varepsilon_0=$$ permittivity of free space or air. The capacitance with a dielectric medium between the plates is

$$C^{\prime}=\frac{\varepsilon_m A}{d} \quad \text{.... (ii)}$$

where, $$\varepsilon_m=$$ permittivity of medium.

Here, $$C^{\prime}=15 \mu \mathrm{F}, C=3 \mu \mathrm{F}$$

and $$\varepsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12}$$ SI unit

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{C^{\prime}}{C} & =\frac{\varepsilon_m A}{d} \times \frac{d}{\varepsilon_0 A}=\frac{\varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon_0} \\ \varepsilon_m & =\frac{\varepsilon_0 C^{\prime}}{C}=\frac{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 15 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 10^{-6}} \\ & =44.25 \times 10^{-12} \text { SI unit } \\ & =0.4425 \times 10^{-10} \text { SI unit } \end{aligned}$$

content: In a parallel plate air capacitor the distance between plates is reduced to one fourth and the space between them is filled with a dielectric medium of constant 2 . If the initial capacity of the capacitor is $4 \mu \mathrm{~F}$. then its new capacity is
explanation:
The capacitance of a parallel plate air capacitor is given by

$$C_0=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\quad\text{.... (i)}$$

where, $\varepsilon_0=$ permittivity of the medium,

$A=$ area of plates

and $\quad d=$ distance between the plates

When the distance between plates is reduced and a dielectric slab is introduced, then the capacitance becomes

$C=\frac{K \varepsilon_0 A}{d_1}\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

Where, $K=$ dielectric constant of medium.

Here, $K=2, d_1=\frac{d}{4}$ and $C_0=4 \mu \mathrm{~F}=4 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$

From Eq. (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} C & =4\left(\frac{K \varepsilon_0 A}{d_1}\right)=4 K\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\right) \\ & =4 K C_0\quad\text{.... (iii) [from Eq. (i)]} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting given values in Eq. (iii), we get

$$C=4 \times 2 \times 4=32 \mu \mathrm{~F}$$

content: Which of the following combinations of 7 identical capacitors each of $2 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ gives a resultant capacitance of $10 / 11 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ ?
explanation:
For $n$ capacitors i.e., $C_4, C_2, \ldots \ldots \ldots . C_n$ their equivalent capacitance, when connected in

(a) series is given as, $\frac{1}{C_s}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\ldots \ldots$

and

(b) parallel is given as, $C_p=C_1+C_2+\ldots \ldots \ldots$

In the given problem, we will check the net capacitance in each option and compare it with the desired capacitance mentioned in it.

(a) 3 capacitors are in parallel and 4 capacitors are connect in series, i.e.,

where, $C_{A B}$ is the equivalent capacitance of parallel branches between $A$ and $B$ and $C_{B D}$ is equivalent capacitance of series capacitors between $B$ and $D$. So, equivalent capacitance.

$$C_{\text {eq }}=\frac{C_{A B} \cdot C_{B D}}{C_{A B}+C_{B D}}=\frac{6 \times \frac{2}{4}}{6+\frac{2}{4}}=\frac{6}{13} \mu \mathrm{~F}$$

Similarly, (b) 2 capacitors are in parallel and 5 capacitors are connect in series i.e.,

So, equivalent capacitance,

$$C_{\text {eq }}=\frac{4 \times \frac{2}{5}}{4+\frac{2}{5}}=\frac{4}{11} \mu \mathrm{~F}$$

(c) 4 capacitors are in parallel and 3 capacitors are connect in series i.e.,

So, equivalent capacitance,

$$C_{\text {eq }}=\frac{8 \times \frac{2}{3}}{8+\frac{2}{3}}=\frac{16}{26}=\frac{8}{13} \mu \mathrm{~F}$$

(d) 5 capacitors are in parallel and 2 capacitors are connect in series i.e.,

So, equivalent capacitance,

$$C_{e q}=\frac{10 \times 1}{10+1}=\frac{10}{11} \mu \mathrm{~F}$$

So, the correct option is (d).
